isolation.serialization: All Flutter runs were executed one at a time with no overlap across devices.
isolation.sharedState: Reused the existing bridge and current simulator/emulator state without killing or restarting shared services.

commandsRun.observation: Passed on iOS; all four reconnect/offline cache cases succeeded, covering first-run pairing to a live thread list, offline cached thread restoration, reconnect controls, and deduplicated reconnect recovery.

Passed on iOS; all seven notification routing and dedup cases succeeded, including approval-detail routing, thread-detail opens, cold-start targets, stale notification suppression, and duplicate event suppression.
Passed on iOS; the real bridge-backed approval queue/detail flow completed through approve and reject actions in the mobile UI, exercising git approval gating against the shared bridge.
Passed on Android; the same four reconnect/offline cache cases succeeded, matching the iOS coverage for pairing into threads, offline readability, reconnect controls, and reconnect deduplication.
Passed on Android; the same seven notification routing and dedup cases succeeded, matching iOS for approval/thread deep links, cold-start routing, stale suppression, and duplicate suppression.

Passed on Android; the real bridge-backed approval queue/detail flow completed through approve and reject actions in the mobile UI using the emulator bridge mapping, with no adb install repair needed.

assertions.title: Core mobile flows behave equivalently on iOS and Android

steps.action: Run reconnect_offline_cache_test.dart with -r expanded on the configured iOS simulator and Android emulator.
steps.expected: Both platforms should prove the same pairing, live thread list, offline cache readability, reconnect controls, and reconnect dedup behavior.
steps.observed: Both iOS and Android passed all 4 reconnect/offline cases; each platform reached a live thread list after pairing, restored cached thread content offline, kept thread detail readable through disconnects, and restored trusted session/thread/approvals/repo context without duplication.

steps.action: Run notification_deep_links_and_dedup_test.dart with -r expanded on both targets.
steps.expected: Both platforms should surface the same foreground notification routing and dedup behavior for approvals and threads.
steps.observed: Both iOS and Android passed all 7 notification cases; approval-detail opens, live thread-detail opens, cold-start launch targets, stale approval suppression, and duplicate notification suppression behaved the same on both targets.

steps.action: Run live_bridge_approval_flow_test.dart with -r expanded on both targets against the shared bridge.
steps.expected: Both platforms should complete the same real mobile approval queue/detail/approve/reject flow backed by live bridge git approval gating.
steps.observed: Both iOS and Android passed the real bridge-backed approval UI flow, including queue access, approval detail rendering, approve/reject actions, and final approved/rejected bridge state, demonstrating parity for approvals and git-control gating.

evidence.consoleErrors: none observed in the expanded Flutter test logs on either platform
evidence.network: The live approval suite exercised the shared bridge at http://127.0.0.1:3110 on iOS and http://10.0.2.2:3110 on Android; reconnect/offline and notification suites used the app integration harness rather than external network capture.
evidence.platformNotes: Parity evidence comes from identical suite files passing on both configured targets: reconnect/offline cache (4 cases), notification routing/dedup (7 cases), and live bridge approval flow (1 case).

frictions.description: Multiple attached Flutter devices were present on the host, so every command needed an explicit -d target to stay inside the assigned simulator/emulator isolation boundary.
frictions.resolution: Ran each suite with the exact configured device ID and serialized all six commands.

frictions.description: The live bridge approval suite needs platform-specific bridge reachability because iOS can use 127.0.0.1 directly while the Android emulator must reach the host bridge through 10.0.2.2.
frictions.resolution: Ran iOS with --dart-define=LIVE_BRIDGE_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:3110 and Android with --dart-define=LIVE_BRIDGE_BASE_URL=http://10.0.2.2:3110.

frictions.description: This validation surface produces per-command Flutter logs rather than standalone simulator/emulator screenshots or videos.
frictions.resolution: Saved one expanded log per suite command under the assigned evidence directory and tied parity conclusions directly to those logs.

summary: Ran 6 serialized Flutter integration suite commands (3 on iOS, 3 on Android). All reconnect/offline, notification routing/dedup, and live bridge approval flows passed on both targets, so VAL-PLATFORM-001 passed with no blockers and no Android adb repair required.
